definition: A critical conceptual unit within the top term domain of the Exegesis. Mentions: 83.
interpretive_note: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel's philosophy—particularly his dialectical view of history and the concept of the 'Absolute Spirit' coming to know itself through human consciousness—is a frequent touchstone in the Exegesis. Dick used Hegelian dialectics (thesis, antithesis, synthesis) to explain the conflict between the oppressive Empire (thesis) and the liberating divine intelligence (antithesis), resulting in a transubstantiated, higher reality (synthesis). Dick also resonated with the idea that the universe is fundamentally a vast, unfolding Mind (the Hegelian Absolute) that is alienating itself into matter only to eventually sublate and remember itself, mirroring his own concept of a fractured God seeking reunification.

Dick used Hegelian dialectics (thesis, antithesis, synthesis) to explain the conflict between the oppressive Empire (thesis) and the liberating divine intelligence (antithesis), resulting in a transubstantiated, higher reality (synthesis). Dick also resonated with the idea that the universe is fundamentally a vast, unfolding Mind (the Hegelian Absolute) that is alienating itself into matter only to eventually sublate and remember itself, mirroring his own concept of a fractured God seeking reunification.
full_description:
Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel's philosophy—particularly his dialectical view of history and the concept of the 'Absolute Spirit' coming to know itself through human consciousness—is a frequent touchstone in the Exegesis. Dick used Hegelian dialectics (thesis, antithesis, synthesis) to explain the conflict between the oppressive Empire (thesis) and the liberating divine intelligence (antithesis), resulting in a transubstantiated, higher reality (synthesis). Dick also resonated with the idea that the universe is fundamentally a vast, unfolding Mind (the Hegelian Absolute) that is alienating itself into matter only to eventually sublate and remember itself, mirroring his own concept of a fractured God seeking reunification.

**Category:** Top Term

**Frequency:** 83 mentions. First appears 1976-09-15.
